article_title: EU Commission Declares DMA Fit for Purpose in First Statutory Review and Pivots Enforcement Toward AI and Cloud

content_preview.title: EU Commission Declares DMA Fit for Purpose in First Statutory Review and Pivots Enforcement Toward AI and Cloud
content_preview.summary: Brussels' first review of the Digital Markets Act concludes the law needs no overhaul, declines to designate generative AI as a new core platform service, and shifts focus toward cloud gatekeeper probes and a Google Android AI specification.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

The European Commission published its first statutory review of the Digital Markets Act on April 28, 2026, concluding that the regulation "remains fit for purpose and has opened up new opportunities for businesses and developers," according to the [European Commission](https://digital-markets-act.ec.europa.eu/review-highlights-digital-markets-act-remains-fit-purpose-and-has-positive-impact-2026-04-28_en). The review, required by the DMA's own text within two years of its core obliga...

editor_notes.content_quality: Strong Analysis piece, 1,339 words. Clear three-part structure (Commission verdict, what's not changing, where enforcement is moving) with a substantive Analysis coda that frames the trade-offs of the Article 6(7) approach. Internal cross-reference to April 14 DMA-preview article verified on disk.

source_verification.source-0_eu_commission: Verified: 'remains fit for purpose and has opened up new opportunities for businesses and developers'; April 28 2026 publication.
source_verification.source-1_techpolicy: Verified: '450 contributions' from consultation; 'remains fit for purpose and does not need substantial revision or modification'; cloud market investigations launched November 2025; designations expected November 2026, conclusion May 2027; Article 7 not extended to social networking; Aline Blankertz (Rebalance Now) 'silent on pressures from the US to weaken enforcement'; calls for 'stronger use of sanctions, including higher fines' and 'interim measures to more quickly tackle anticompetitive behavior'.
source_verification.source-2_theregister: Verified: Teresa Ribera EVP for Clean Just Competitive Transition; 'The DMA was designed to be future-proof and adapt to emerging challenges, for example in AI and cloud'; specification proceeding initiated January 2026, preliminary findings sent April 28, public-consultation deadline May 13; Ribera 'Today's proposed measures will give more choice to Android users'; Clare Kelly (Google senior competition counsel — correctly identified as Google in the snapshot, not Apple) quote 'unwarranted intervention' / 'mandate access to sensitive hardware and device permissions'.
source_verification.source-3_euronews: Verified: WhatsApp interoperability example; broader background framing; the DMA has been applicable since May 2023 and covers seven companies.

editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All quoted material traced verbatim. Clare Kelly correctly attributed to Google senior competition counsel (the bot's confidence about this is correct — full context in the snapshot reads 'Google senior competition counsel Clare Kelly'). Ribera title and quotes verified verbatim.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and analytical. The Analysis section labels the Article 6(7) approach as having 'trade-offs' rather than endorsing or condemning it, and characterizes industry/civil-society responses without taking sides.
editor_notes.originality: Builds on April 14 DMA-preview article which was the first preview of this review; the cross-reference is correct and the angle is genuinely new (the published outcome rather than the preview).
editor_notes.overall_assessment: APPROVE. Cryptography, sources, content, and quoting discipline all clean. Solid Analysis piece with proper cross-reference.
